turns.content: I'm trying to generate a unique ID for my student project using the nanoid crate, but I keep getting this error: "cannot find value for character '€' in allowed characters". Can you help me figure out what went wrong?

It looks like you tried to include a non-URL-safe character from your custom alphabet into the generated ID, which violates the crate's constraints. The nanoid crate enforces strict rules about the output string based on the configuration passed to its generation function. When you define an allowed set of characters, every byte in the resulting ID must come from that specific set; if you accidentally include a character like '€' or any other symbol outside of ASCII 0-255 that isn't part of your alphabet definition, the crate will reject it immediately to ensure the output remains URL-safe. The core issue here is likely that you assumed Rust strings could hold any Unicode without validation, but nanoid requires explicit control over every character used in the final output.

Oh I see, so I just need to remove those special characters? But wait, I thought nanoid allowed me to pick a custom alphabet entirely. Why am I getting restricted if I'm supposed to be able to define my own set?

You're right that nanoid supports custom alphabets via its configuration trait parameters, but the restriction you hit comes from how those characters are mapped to bits in memory during generation. The crate doesn't just accept a list of allowed symbols; it validates the entire output stream against your defined alphabet before returning anything. If your custom alphabet included '€', the system would try to encode that character into the ID, but since nanoid expects only valid URL-safe bytes, it blocks the operation entirely rather than silently failing or producing invalid data. The crate ensures that every byte in the resulting string is derived strictly from your provided character set, so if any of your choices fall outside the safe subset, the generation will fail before you even see a result.

Ah okay, so I need to make sure my alphabet only contains valid URL-safe characters? That makes sense now. So the real mistake was thinking that defining an alphabet meant I could use whatever unicode I wanted without checking if it fits the nanoid format?

Exactly. The assumption you made early on—that any character can be part of a custom alphabet for this crate—is the common beginner error. Nanoid isn't just a random ID generator; it's a strict formatter that guarantees output compatibility with web standards by design. When you set up your configuration, every symbol in your allowed list must already satisfy the URL-safe requirement, otherwise the entire generation process short-circuits to prevent you from getting broken IDs. Once you restrict your alphabet to only ASCII characters that aren't whitespace or newlines, the crate will generate valid nanoid strings automatically without any further errors.
